sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: DOWN, RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 368, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Player at (28,39) small, blocked from moving DOWN. 'G' at (28,40). 'P' bar at 11.
- Plan: Check ascii around player to understand blocking, then try UP.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 367 actions; a strong score needs about 71 or fewer. You are 5.2x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.content:
Now I can see the ascii layout:
Row 28: bNNNWGGGGcc
Row 29: gNNNWGbbGcb
Row 30: gNNNWGbbGcb
Row 31: bNNNWGGGGcc
The player 'W' is at (28, 39). Looking at the ascii:
Row 28: ...WGGGGcc
Row 29: ...WGbbGcb
Row 30: ...WGbbGcb
Row 31: ...WGGGGcc
Wait, the player is at (28, 39), which is the 'W' in row 28. The 'G' objects are at cols 40-43 in row 28.
Looking at row 29, the 'G' is at col 40, then 'bb' at cols 41-42, then 'G' at col 43.
So the layout is:
- Row 28: W at col 39, GGGG at cols 40-43
- Row 29: W at col 39 (part of player), G at col 40, bb at cols 41-42, G at col 43
- Row 30: W at col 39 (part of player), G at col 40, bb at cols 41-42, G at col 43
- Row 31: W at col 39 (part of player), GGGG at cols 40-43
Wait, the player is small (4 pixels), so it's a 2x2 square. The player at (28, 39) covers (28, 39), (28, 40), (29, 39), (29, 40).
But looking at the ascii, row 28 has 'W' at col 39 and 'G' at col 40. Row 29 has 'W' at col 39 and 'G' at col 40.
So the player covers (28, 39) and (29, 39), which is a 2x1 vertical strip. But the player has 4 pixels, so it should be 2x2.
Actually, looking more carefully at the ascii:
Row 28: ...WGGGGcc
Row 29: ...WGbbGcb
The 'W' is at col 39 in both rows 28 and 29. But the player has 4 pixels, so it should cover 2x2 cells. If the player is at (28, 39), it covers (28, 39), (28, 40), (29, 39), (29, 40).
But the ascii shows 'W' at (28, 39) and 'G' at (28, 40). This means the player doesn't cover (28, 40).
Wait, maybe the player is a 2x2 square at (28, 39), covering (28, 39), (28, 40), (29, 39), (29, 40). But the ascii shows 'G' at (28, 40) and (29, 40). This is confusing.
Actually, I think the segmentation is showing the player as a separate object from the 'G' object, even though they overlap in the ascii view. The 'W' in the ascii view represents the player, and the 'G' represents the 'G' object.
Looking at the ascii more carefully:
Row 28: ...WGGGGcc
Row 29: ...WGbbGcb
The 'W' is at col 39 in both rows. The 'G' is at col 40 in both rows. So the player is a 2x1 vertical strip at col 39, rows 28-29.
But the player has 4 pixels, which would be a 2x2 square. So the player should cover cols 39-40, rows 28-29.
But the ascii shows 'G' at col 40, which means the 'G' object is overlapping with the player's position.
I think the ascii view is showing the top-most object at each cell. So the 'W' at (28, 39) and (29, 39) is the player, and the 'G' at (28, 40) and (29, 40) is the 'G' object.
This means the player is a 2x1 vertical strip at col 39, rows 28-29. But the player has 4 pixels, so it should be 2x2.
Wait, maybe the player is a 2x2 square at (28, 39), covering (28, 39), (28, 40), (29, 39), (29, 40). But the ascii shows 'G' at (28, 40) and (29, 40), which means the 'G' object is on top of the player at those cells.
This is confusing. Let me just try to move the player UP and see what happens.
Actually, looking at the ascii, the player is blocked from moving DOWN because there's a 'G' at (29, 40) and (30, 40). The player at (28, 39) would move to (29, 39) if it moves DOWN. But the 'G' at (29, 40) is adjacent to the player's new position.
Wait, the player is at (28, 39), and it tried to move DOWN. The player would move to (29, 39). But the ascii shows 'W' at (29, 39), which means the player is already at (29, 39).
I'm confused about the player's position. Let me re-examine.
The segmentation says the player 'W' is at (28, 39) with 4 pixels. The ascii shows 'W' at (28, 39) and (29, 39). So the player is a 2x1 vertical strip at col 39, rows 28-29.
But the player has 4 pixels, so it should be 2x2. Maybe the player is a 2x2 square at (28, 39), covering (28, 39), (28, 40), (29, 39), (29, 40). But the ascii shows 'G' at (28, 40) and (29, 40), which means the 'G' object is on top of the player at those cells.
Actually, I think the segmentation is correct. The player is at (28, 39) with 4 pixels. The ascii shows 'W' at (28, 39) and (29, 39), which is 2 cells. But the player has 4 pixels, so there must be 2 more 'W' cells that are not visible in the ascii because they're covered by the 'G' object.
OK, I think I understand now. The player is a 2x2 square at (28, 39), covering (28, 39), (28, 40), (29, 39), (29, 40). The 'G' object is at (28, 40), covering (28, 40) to (31, 43) or similar. The 'G' object overlaps with the player at (28, 40) and (29, 40).
The ascii view shows the top-most object at each cell. So the 'W' at (28, 39) and (29, 39) is the player, and the 'G' at (28, 40) and (29, 40) is the 'G' object (on top of the player).
This means the player is partially covered by the 'G' object. The player can't move RIGHT because the 'G' is in the way. The player can't move DOWN because the 'G' extends to row 30 and 31.
Let me try to move UP. The player at (28, 39) would move to (27, 39). Row 27 is all 'w' (background), so there should be nothing blocking the player from moving UP.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Player at (28,39) small, partially covered by 'G' at (28,40). 'G' blocks RIGHT and DOWN. 'P' bar at 11.
Plan: Try moving UP to get out from under the 'G'.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>

# Player blocked by G. Try UP
result = action(['UP'])
print(f"UP result: gameplay_changed={last_action_result.get('gameplay_changed')}, level_completed={last_action_result.get('level_completed')}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
print("\nKey objects:")
for n in nodes:
    if n['pixels'] <= 30:
        b = n['boundary']
        r_min = min(r for r,c in b)
        c_min = min(c for r,c in b)
        print(f"  {n['color']}: pos=({r_min},{c_min}), pixels={n['pixels']}")

# Check ascii around player
lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("\nAscii around player:")
for r in range(26, 32):
    print(f"Row {r}: {lines[r][35:46]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
